subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! You already know that a fraction is a way of showing part of a whole. In this unit you will become much more confident with fractions and learn four powerful new skills. You will discover equivalent fractions (different-looking fractions that are really worth the same), you will compare fractions to say which is bigger, you will find a fraction of an amount (like 1/4 of 20 mangoes), and you will add and subtract fractions that share the same bottom number.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what equivalent fractions are and find them by multiplying or dividing.
Show equivalent fractions with pictures such as fraction strips and bars.
Compare two fractions and use the symbols >, < and =.
Compare fractions with the same denominator and with the same numerator.
Use benchmark fractions like 0, 1/2 and 1 to judge size.
Find a fraction of an amount by dividing then multiplying.
Add and subtract fractions that have the same denominator.
Simplify a fraction to its lowest terms after adding or subtracting.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions
concepts.explanation: Equivalent fractions are different fractions that show the same amount of a whole. They may look different on the page, but they are worth exactly the same. This is one of the most useful ideas in all of fractions. Picture a warm anjero, the flat bread many families eat. If Farah cuts it into 2 equal pieces and takes 1, she has eaten 1/2 of the anjero. Now picture the same anjero cut into 4 equal pieces. If she takes 2 of those pieces, she has eaten 2/4. Look at her plate both times – it is the very same amount of…
concepts.example: We can keep going. Cut the anjero into 6 equal pieces and take 3, and you have 3/6 – still one half. Cut it into 8 and take 4, and you have 4/8 – still one half. So 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8. All of these are equivalent,…

concepts.title: Simplifying Fractions
concepts.explanation: Simplifying a fraction means writing it in its lowest terms – using the smallest numbers possible while keeping the same value. To simplify, you divide the numerator and denominator by the same number. Take 4/8. Both 4 and 8 can be divided by 4: 4÷4 = 1 and 8÷4 = 2, so 4/8 = 1/2. The fraction 1/2 is the simplest name for that amount. Take 6/9: both 6 and 9 can be divided by 3, giving 2/3, so 6/9 = 2/3.
concepts.example: A fraction is in its lowest terms when the numerator and denominator share no common factor except 1. For example, 3/5 is already simplest, because 3 and 5 have nothing in common except 1. Simplifying is very handy…

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions
concepts.explanation: To compare two fractions is to decide which one is bigger, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. There are several neat ways to do this, and you choose the easiest one for the fractions in front of you. Same denominator: look at the numerators
concepts.example: When two fractions have the same denominator (same bottom number), comparing is easy: the fraction with the bigger numerator is bigger. This makes sense – if the pieces are all the same size, then whoever has more…

concepts.title: Finding a Fraction of an Amount
concepts.explanation: Finding a fraction of an amount means working out a part of a whole number of things. For example, finding 1/4 of 20 means splitting 20 into 4 equal groups and taking 1 group. This skill is used every day when you share things fairly. Divide the amount by the denominator (the bottom number).
concepts.example: Multiply your answer by the numerator (the top number).

concepts.title: Adding Fractions with the Same Denominator
concepts.explanation: When two fractions have the same denominator, adding them is simple: add the numerators and keep the denominator the same. The denominator does not change because the pieces are all the same size – you are just counting how many pieces you have in total. To add 1/5 + 2/5: add the numerators, 1 + 2 = 3, and keep the denominator 5. So 1/5 + 2/5 = 3/5. Think of a bariis dish cut into 5 equal slices. If you take 1 slice and then 2 more, you have 3 slices out of 5 – that is 3/5.
concepts.example: Picture 1/4 + 2/4. Shade 1 quarter of a bar, then shade 2 more quarters. Altogether 3 quarters are shaded, so 1/4 + 2/4 = 3/4. The bar was always in quarters, so the bottom number stayed 4 throughout.

concepts.title: Subtracting Fractions with the Same Denominator
concepts.explanation: Subtracting fractions with the same denominator works the same friendly way: subtract the numerators and keep the denominator the same. To work out 4/6 - 1/6: subtract the numerators, 4 - 1 = 3, and keep the denominator 6. So 4/6 - 1/6 = 3/6, which simplifies to 1/2. If a jug holds 6 equal cups of water and is filled to 4/6, and you pour out 1/6, then 3/6 (a half) is still in the jug.
concepts.example: Picture 3/8 - 1/8. Start with 3 of 8 equal parts shaded, then rub out 1 part. You are left with 2 shaded parts, so 3/8 - 1/8 = 2/8, which simplifies to 1/4. Again the denominator stays 8, because the whole is always in…

methods.title: How to find a fraction of an amount
steps: Divide the amount by the denominator (bottom number).
Multiply your answer by the numerator (top number).
Check the how to find a fraction of an amount result carefully.

methods.title: How to compare two fractions
steps: If the denominators match, compare the numerators.
If the numerators match, the smaller denominator wins.
Otherwise, change both to the same denominator and compare.

methods.title: How to simplify a fraction
steps: Find a number that divides both the top and the bottom.
Divide both by that number.
Repeat until the only common factor left is 1.

methods.title: Compare two fractions
steps: Solution: Use a common denominator of 15.

methods.title: Find a fraction of an amount
methods.example: In a class of 30 pupils at Nuur Academy, 2/5 walk to school. How many walk?
steps: Solution: Divide 30 ÷ 5 = 6, then multiply by 2: 6 × 2 = 12.
So 12 pupils walk to school.
Check the find a fraction of an amount result carefully.

methods.title: Add fractions
methods.example: Work out 3/8 + 2/8 and simplify if you can.
steps: Solution: Add the numerators: 3 + 2 = 5.
Keep the denominator 8.
Since 5 and 8 share no common factor, it is already in lowest terms.

workedExamples.title: Find three equivalent fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply top and bottom by the same number each time. ×2 gives 4/6; ×3 gives 6/9; ×4 gives 8/12. So 2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9 = 8/12. Each shows the same amount.

workedExamples.title: Fill in the missing number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominator went from 5 to 10, which is ×2. So multiply the top by 2 as well: 3 × 2 = 6. The missing number is 6, so 3/5 = 6/10.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both 10 and 15 divide by 5. 10 ÷ 5 = 2 and 15 ÷ 5 = 3. So 10/15 = 2/3, which cannot be simplified further.

workedExamples.title: Compare two fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use a common denominator of 15. 3/5 = 9/15 (×3) and 2/3 = 10/15 (×5). Since 10/15 > 9/15, 2/3 > 3/5.

workedExamples.title: Find a fraction of an amount
workedExamples.prompt: In a class of 30 pupils at Nuur Academy, 2/5 walk to school. How many walk?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide 30 ÷ 5 = 6, then multiply by 2: 6 × 2 = 12. So 12 pupils walk to school.

workedExamples.title: Add fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 3/8 + 2/8 and simplify if you can.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the numerators: 3 + 2 = 5. Keep the denominator 8. So 3/8 + 2/8 = 5/8. Since 5 and 8 share no common factor, it is already in lowest terms.

workedExamples.title: Subtract fractions
workedExamples.prompt: A jug is 5/6 full. Halima pours out 2/6 of the jug. What fraction is still filled?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the numerators: 5 - 2 = 3. Keep the denominator 6. So 5/6 - 2/6 = 3/6, which simplifies to 1/2. Half the jug is still filled.

workedExamples.title: Add to make a whole
workedExamples.prompt: Amina eats 2/8 of a mango pie and Sahra eats 3/8. What fraction did they eat together?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 2/8 + 3/8 = 5/8. So together they ate 5/8 of the pie, and 3/8 was left over.

workedExamples.title: Add and simplify where possible
workedExamples.prompt: Add and simplify where possible: a) 2/8 + 2/8 b) 2/6 + 3/6 c) 1/4 + 1/4.

workedExamples.title: Subtract and simplify where possible
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract and simplify where possible: a) 4/6 - 1/6 b) 6/8 - 2/8.

workedExamples.title: Fill in to make equivalent fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Fill in to make equivalent fractions: a) 3/5 = □/10 b) 2/3 = 8/□ c) □/4 = 3/12.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Which is greater, 2/3 or 3/4? Convert both to twelfths and explain.
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Fraction Strips
activities.materials: strips of paper of equal length, a pencil, and a ruler.
steps: Take four strips of the same length. Keep the first whole and label it 1.
Fold the second into 2 equal parts; label each part 1/2.
Fold the third into 4 equal parts; label each 1/4. Fold the fourth into 8; label each 1/8.
Lay the strips under each other. Line up 1/2 against the quarters and eighths – what equivalent fractions can you see?
You should find that 1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8. Keep your strips to use all through this unit.

activities.title: Fold and Share the Anjero
activities.materials: a round piece of paper (a pretend anjero) and a pencil.
steps: Fold your paper anjero in half, then in half again, to make 4 equal parts.
Shade 2 of the 4 parts. What fraction is shaded? Is it the same as 1/2?
Now fold a fresh anjero into 8 equal parts and shade 4. Is this still one half?
Explain to a family member why 2/4 and 4/8 are both equal to 1/2.

activities.title: The Fraction Compare Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each with one fraction written on it (for example 1/2, 2/5, 3/4, 2/3, 3/8).
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down.
Each player turns over one card.
Compare the two fractions – whoever has the bigger fraction wins both cards.
If it is tricky, change both fractions to the same denominator to be sure. Play until the cards run out.
This game trains you to compare fractions quickly and fairly.

activities.title: Fraction of a Set (Counting Investigation)
activities.materials: 24 small objects such as stones, beans or bottle caps.
steps: Count out 24 objects.
Find 1/3 of them: share the objects into 3 equal groups and count one group.
Find 2/3 of them: count two of the groups.
Try again with different numbers, such as 1/4 of 20 or 3/5 of 15. Write down each answer.

activities.title: Fraction Addition Game (Family or Group)
activities.materials: cards with same-denominator fractions (for example many eighths and many sixths), paper and a pencil.
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal two to each player, making sure both share a denominator.
Each player adds or subtracts their two fractions and writes the answer.
Simplify the answer if you can – earn a bonus point for simplifying correctly.
The player with the most points after several rounds wins.

activities.title: Kitchen Fraction Hunt
activities.materials: a helpful adult and safe kitchen items (a measuring cup, a bowl of dates).
steps: Find something you can measure in fractions, such as a 1/4 cup or a 1/2 cup.
Use the 1/4 cup twice – how many quarters make one half? Show that 2/4 = 1/2.
Count a bowl of dates and find 1/2 and then 1/4 of them by sharing into groups.
Tell your family one place where fractions are used in cooking.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand equivalent fractions best? What was the trickiest part of comparing or adding fractions? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Equivalent Fractions Rule
rules.text: Multiply (or divide) the top and bottom by the SAME number. 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8. Adding the same number to top and bottom does NOT work.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: the bigger numerator is the bigger fraction. Same numerator: the smaller denominator is the bigger fraction. Otherwise: change to the same denominator, then compare.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: add or subtract the numerators only. Keep the denominator the same. Simplify the answer if you can.

terms: The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts
Different fractions with the same value
Write a fraction in its lowest terms
A known fraction (0, 1/2, 1) used to compare
A part of a whole number, e.g. 1/4 of 20
commonMistakes: Multiply top and bottom by the same number
Keep the denominator; add only the numerators
Divide top and bottom by 4 to get 1/2

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A jug is 5/6 full. If 2/6 of the jug is poured out, what fraction is still filled? Simplify your answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Halima says 1/4 is bigger than 1/3 because 4 is bigger than 3. Is she right? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Subtracting Fractions with the Same Denominator.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Subtracting fractions with the same denominator works the same friendly way: subtract the numerators and keep the denominator the same. To work out 4/6 - 1/6: subtract the numerators, 4 - 1 = 3, and keep the denominator 6. So 4/6 - 1/6 = 3/6, which simplifies to 1/2. If a jug holds 6 equal cups of water and is filled to 4/6, and you pour out 1/6, then 3/6 (a half) is still in the jug.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

selfAssessment: I can explain what equivalent fractions are and find them by multiplying or dividing.
I can show equivalent fractions with pictures such as fraction strips and bars.
I can compare two fractions and use the symbols >, < and =.
I can compare fractions with the same denominator and with the same numerator.
I can use benchmark fractions like 0, 1/2 and 1 to judge size.
I can find a fraction of an amount by dividing then multiplying.
I can add and subtract fractions that have the same denominator.
I can simplify a fraction to its lowest terms after adding or subtracting.
